sevenDayForecast.headline: Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle to impact Far North Queensland on Friday and eastern Northern Territory on the weekend.
points: Tropical Cyclone Advices and Forecast Track Maps are being issued and should be referred to for more detailed information
Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle is moving west towards the eastern Cape York Peninsula coast. There is high confidence that it will continue to move west over the coming days.

Narelle is forecast to track towards and cross the far northern Queensland coast during Friday, before continuing into the Gulf of Carpentaria, where there is also a High chance it will be a tropical cyclone, and impacting the Northern Territory over the weekend.
Early next week Narelle may move offshore of the Kimberley coast where it has a High chance of re-developing into a tropical cyclone.

trackForecast.headline: Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle (34U) remains category 5, moving steadily towards the Queensland coast and then into the Gulf of Carpentaria.
trackForecast.discussion:
Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle has intensified to a category 5 system. It has been located using satellite imagery with good confidence. The environment is favourable and Narelle is likely to maintain category 5 intensity as it moves west, possibly weakening slightly prior to landfall on the Cape York Peninsula, Queensland, most likely during Friday morning.

There is high confidence in the track of Narelle and it is expected to move steadily west. The system is expected to stay well north of Abbot Point with no threat of any direct wind impacts at the location.

Over the weekend, Narelle will move into the Gulf of Carpentaria where it is likely to re-strengthen into a severe tropical cyclone. The current forecast track suggests it will pass close to Groote Eylandt on Saturday afternoon or evening, most likely as a category 3 system, but there is approximately a 25% risk that it will be a stronger category 4 system at this time.
trackForecast.discussionUpperBound: The upper bound reflects the system developing faster than anticipated and remaining over water for longer.
